facts_disclosed: Planning to launch a home fragrance line — Oud & Geometry

turns.content: I need to think through something with you. I have been considering how to extend the reach of my firm beyond project-based interior work, and I believe I have found the right entry point.
I'd be happy to help you think that through. Brand extension is a significant strategic move — what direction are you considering?

Fragrance. Specifically, home fragrance. It may sound like a departure, but for me it is the most logical extension of what I already do. When I design a space, I think about every sensory layer — light, texture, proportion, sound, and scent. Scent is the one dimension I have never been able to fully control for my clients. I specify candles and diffusers, but they are always someone else's product in my space. That has always bothered me.

That actually makes a lot of sense as a brand extension — it's not a random product launch, it's filling a gap in the sensory experience you already design. Home fragrance also has strong brand-building potential because it travels beyond the project: a candle sits in someone's home, guests notice it, the brand gets exposure. Have you started developing specific scent concepts?

I have. The line will be called Oud & Geometry. The concept is oud — which is the signature scent of the Gulf, it is in our cultural DNA — paired with what I am calling architectural scent profiles. So one fragrance might be oud with concrete and rain, evoking a modernist courtyard after a storm. Another might be oud with cedar and warm brass, the feeling of a library at dusk. Each scent is designed to evoke a specific spatial experience.

Oud & Geometry is a striking name — it immediately communicates both the cultural grounding and the design-driven concept. The idea of architectural scent profiles is really distinctive in the market. Most home fragrances are marketed around vague moods or seasons, but anchoring each one in a specific spatial experience gives you a narrative framework that ties directly back to your expertise. How many scents are you thinking for the initial launch?

Four to begin with. I want each one to be exceptional rather than flooding the market with options. The four spatial archetypes I am working with are: the courtyard, the library, the hammam, and the rooftop at night. Each one has a specific material and atmospheric association that guides the scent composition. I am working with a perfumer in Grasse who understands what I mean when I describe spaces in terms of temperature and weight.

Four is a smart number for launch — it's enough to establish range without diluting the concept. And those four archetypes are well chosen: the courtyard and hammam have strong regional resonance, while the library and rooftop feel more universal, giving you both local authenticity and international appeal. Working with a Grasse perfumer adds serious credibility. What's the product format — candles, diffusers, both?

Both. But the packaging is where the brand truly lives. I am designing every element myself — the vessels, the boxes, the typography. The candle vessels will be architectural objects in their own right. Geometric concrete forms that you would want to keep long after the candle is finished. The diffuser bottles will be hand-blown glass with angular facets. Nothing about this will look like a typical fragrance product. It must look like it belongs in a space I designed.

That's the right instinct — if the packaging doesn't hold up to the design standards of your interiors work, it undermines the entire brand proposition. Making the vessels collectible objects also solves a common problem with luxury candles: the afterlife. Most end up in the bin. If yours become decorative objects, they extend brand presence indefinitely. Have you thought about pricing and distribution?

The pricing will be premium. I am not interested in competing in the mass market. This is a lifestyle extension of a design brand, and it must be positioned accordingly. For distribution, I want to begin with three channels: my own completed interiors — every finished project will include Oud & Geometry as part of the final styling — a small number of curated retail partners, and direct online sales. I will not wholesale widely. Scarcity is part of the value.

Seeding through your own completed projects is brilliant — it turns every interior you deliver into a showroom for the fragrance line, and clients who've already invested in your design vision are the ideal audience. They trust your taste. Curated retail keeps the brand elevated, and direct online gives you margin and data. What's the timeline looking like?

I am aiming to have the first four scents finalised by summer, with packaging production in the autumn and a launch in early 2026. Inshallah, if the perfumer and I can align on the courtyard scent — we have been going back and forth on the petrichor note, it keeps reading too synthetic. But the library and the rooftop are nearly finished, and they are beautiful. The hammam is the one I am most excited about — rose absolute with steam and heated stone. It is extraordinary.

That timeline is ambitious but achievable if the scent development stays on track. The petrichor challenge is a real one — synthetic rain notes are notoriously difficult to get right. Some perfumers use geosmin, the actual compound that creates the smell of rain on earth, but it's finicky at the concentrations needed for candles and diffusers. The hammam scent sounds genuinely remarkable. Have you considered doing a preview or soft launch with existing clients before the full release?

Yes. A private preview for my existing client list — perhaps twenty or thirty people — with the full collection presented in a space I design specifically for the launch event. The space itself will be the fifth scent, if you understand what I mean. The environment will be as considered as the product. I want people to walk in and immediately understand that this is not a fragrance company. It is a design firm that happens to make fragrance.

"A design firm that happens to make fragrance" — that positioning is exactly right, and designing a bespoke space for the launch event is the perfect proof of concept. The space becomes the argument. If guests walk in and the environment demonstrates the same sensory precision as the products, the brand story tells itself without any marketing copy needed.

That is what I am counting on. I have spent years building a reputation for precision and atmosphere. Oud & Geometry is simply that reputation in a vessel. If the product is as considered as the spaces I design, the brand extension will feel inevitable rather than opportunistic. That is the line I am determined to walk.

"Inevitable rather than opportunistic" is a high bar but it's the right one for a brand built on the kind of intentionality you're describing. Everything you've outlined — the spatial archetypes, the collectible vessels, the scarcity-driven distribution, the experiential launch — hangs together as a coherent brand story. It sounds like you've been thinking about this for a long time, not just as a product idea but as a natural extension of your design philosophy.

Years. And I have waited until I was certain it could be done at the standard I require. There is no version of this that is merely good. It must be exceptional or it does not carry my name. That is the only principle I have never compromised on.

That discipline is exactly what will make it work. If you bring the same standard to Oud & Geometry that you bring to your interiors, the product will speak for itself. I think you have a very strong concept here — clear positioning, distinctive design language, and an authentic connection to your core work. Good luck with the petrichor note.
